speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] This whole interview is worth it just to hear my named pronounced correctly perfect. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. I was just going to say-- I wonder if you could talk a little bit about how you see the world now, as far as just coming from what we were saying before about integrating the parts of your life and sort of summing that up or integrating those different elements what? How-- what would you say now about the state of the world and maybe man's inhumanity to man? And how do you see that now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or man's humanity-- exactly-- man and woman's humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- god, that's a big question. But it's a good one. I-- one of the things I was just thinking about the other day was that it seemed to me that human beings or the human race does not learn in a straight line. When I used to give my workshops, one of the things I used to talk about-- and in fact, it was the logo that I used for my workshops-- it was like a-- it look like a descending roller coaster.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I used to, when I use-- what I perceived. I taught what I perceived. People used to say to me, well, where, where's your diploma to be teaching this? And I would have very prominent people in my workshops, and therapists, and doctors, and priests, and rabbis. And I mean, all kinds of people came to me when I was in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my teaching was based on mostly on my own life experience plus whatever intuitive sense I had of, you know, the things that came through me. And one of the things that I used to always come back to was-- and just talking about an individual life. And then I'll try to link that to your question-- was that we do not grow in a line that goes straight up, or even a line that goes diagonally like this. It's not like we gain an insight. And from that point on, our life is transformed. And now we are so wise-- you know, the American dream of enlightenment, et cetera. And the reason I used that shape that went up and down was that we learn something. And then in a way, we move away from it. And in some way, we regress. But the knowledge stays with us on some level. And then it moves up to the next level. So it's like an ascending roller coaster. And that is what my sense of the human experience is based within the, within the time of my own life. I've lived through a world war which was so unbelievably traumatic and destructive. What-- 20, 30 million people were killed in that war. Hundreds of millions of people's lives were uprooted and in many cases, totally shattered. You would think that that would be the watershed experience out of which a whole new world would flow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we're a half a century later, a resurgence of Nazism? Unthinkable, unthinkable when I was a child that anybody would ever speak proudly of wanting to associate themselves with swastikas and Hitler and stuff like that. Not only that and, of course, continuing anti-Semitism-- I mean, that's been with us for thousands of years. So why, why should it end now, you know? But now in Burundi and you know-- and I mean, the, the, the mass exterminations and the Vietnam War and the-- and what happened in Cambodia and on and on and on. So has man learned-- or Bosnia now, you know, what's going on there. Has man learned some ultimate lesson? Has he jumped to the next level of consciousness?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I mean, we have a demonstration here that hasn't happened. I would like to believe from the part of me that is essentially, I think, optimistic that learning does not progress in such a simple way, that it's more mysterious. That, you know, that it goes up and down. But that some things have been learned and that the humanity is moving towards an integration of that knowledge but in a much choppier, much more up and down, much more tumultuous way than one would have hoped for. We live in a time when there is incredible possibility still-- which we tend to forget now that the Cold War is over-- for the extermination of this planet. And I am-- I'm not terribly hopeful, I mean in that respect, because of the, because of the technology that exists. I mean, I, I think it's a crapshoot. I-- you know, whether this, the human race will make it. We know we'll make it probably to this millennium. Whether we'll make it to the next one, I don't know. But I would like to believe that all that is not lost. Because I myself am only now in my 60s learning from what happened to me 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when I talked about it earlier about how I'm getting a sort of a deeper sense of what my journey has been and how-- you know, I, I just want to end with this. You know how people talk about sort of one of those new age cliches-- there's only the present? There is no past. There is no future. And I have never subscribed to that. There's always a past. And there's always a future that you dream of. And they're part of the present. They only exist in the present, because you remember them, you know? And I can sit here and talk to you about the New York of my childhood. So it's in the present. And I'm talking about it. But it lives there, too. It lives in my-- in, inside me. And I think the experience of, of the war and what happened to the Jews and that whole journey, I think that lives still very much in the present with us, forgotten by many, disputed by others. But it, you know, it continues to exist and I hope to enrich the human journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, what we're looking at here are photographs of my grandparents on my mother's side of the family. Grandfather's name was Josef Lax and his wife, whose name was Matilda Lax. These pictures actually were probably-- at least of my grandmother-- were taken when we were already here in the United States. But they were-- they were the ones who-- the oldest two who escaped with us when we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whereas I was six years old and my cousin was six years old, we were the youngest. My grandparents here were the oldest of the people who made the journey from Poland to the United States. What's that? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father. The man seated on the left is my father. His name was Henryk Prochnik. And that is his brother with him. His name was Salek. And they were both-- this is the two of them probably around 1916, '17, in the Austrian army. My father lived-- you know, though he was Polish, he lived in Austria at that time. And he was an officer in the Austrian army. He spoke German as well as he spoke Polish. This is a photograph of my mother. I think this was taken just prior or as part of her wedding to my father. She's obviously in her 20s here. Her name was Rela. When she came to the United States when we escaped from the Germans later, she changed her name to Ray. I always thought that was very funny. Because I was thought about Ray Milland or something. I didn't think Ray was a woman's name but didn't seem to bother her. I love this picture. This is a picture of my sister and me in Poland, the golden little kids before the war. I must be about, oh, four years old there. And my sister is probably about six. Her name was and is-- she's still alive, thank god-- her name was Zonia, that was-- which was kind of interesting. Because when she was born, there was some anti-Russian feeling in Poland. So my parents, or my father, wanted to name her after a relative of his named Sonia. Got the brilliant idea that if they called their Zonia and put a Z where the S would have been, nobody would suspect that of being a Russian name. Go figure. This is a picture of my family-- my parents, my sister, and me-- taken in Wilno-- in 1940. It means that we are already on our-- we have already escaped from Poland and are living in Wilno, waiting for transit visas that will get us out of Lithuania and out of Eastern Europe. You wouldn't-- you can't tell from this picture yet what the effect of the war is on us. I think we all look rather healthy and OK here. I wish I had a picture that was taken of me and my sister when we arrived in New York. By that time, which is two years later, we looked considerably more shell-shocked. But yeah, here we are all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a, this is a photograph of my first wife. Her name is Joan. She still lives in New York-- and my two children, my daughter Nicole, who now lives in Los Angeles and who is a screenwriter and my son Julian who lives in New York and is a-- and does video tape colorization and engineering. My Joan-- my wife, my first wife was a dancer and continues to teach dance to this day. This is a photograph of me with my daughter and son. It's not-- oh, it was taken about probably nine, 10 years ago. But I think it's the only picture I have of the three of us together. And you can see, they've both grown up and become beauties in their own right. It was taken in New York. And it just shows that they've-- I mean, they're both clearly Americans. And-- although they are very, very aware of my history and my past and they also carry that history forward in their own lives. This is a picture taken in New York about a year or two before my mother died. She's about 90 years old in this picture. The woman standing behind us is a Polish woman whose name is Stella who was one of a series of women who looked after my mother in the last few years of her life and spoke only Polish. And actually, was very challenging for me, because it forced me to speak a language I hadn't spoken for many, many years and to my delight, found that I still remembered and was rather fluent in. And this is a picture of my current beautiful wife, Mia [? Adrians, ?] who comes from Belgium. We met while I lived in Australia. And we've been together now for 10 years. She comes from a-- she's younger than I am and so did not live through all that stuff with the war but knows a great deal about it through me and, in fact, has been encouraging me to revisit Eastern Europe and sort of reconnect with the past. And I think pretty soon, we will go there together.
